title: Between Two Castles of Mad King Ludwig

component_identification.content:
#### Room Tiles
- **147 room tiles** across 7 room types:
  - **Living rooms** (orange) — score based on adjacency to specific room types
  - **Sleeping rooms** (dark blue) — score based on being grouped together or on specific floors
  - **Food rooms** (yellow) — score based on position below or adjacent to other types
  - **Outdoor rooms** (green) — score based on being on the top floor or edges of the castle
  - **Corridor rooms** (grey) — score based on being in a row or connecting other rooms
  - **Utility rooms** (light blue/cyan) — score based on quantity of specific room types in the castle
  - **Downstairs rooms** (purple) — basement rooms that score based on being below the throne room level or on the ground/basement floor
- Each room tile shows its name, room type (color-coded), and scoring condition

#### Specialty Rooms
- Specialty rooms have unique scoring conditions printed on them (e.g., "1 VP per sleeping room in this castle," "2 VP per outdoor room adjacent to this room")
- These are shuffled into the general room tile supply

#### Throne Room Tiles
- **1 throne room tile per castle** — the starting centerpiece tile for every castle
- Throne rooms have no room type and do not score on their own

#### Bonus Cards
- **42 bonus cards** — awarded between Round 1 and Round 2
- Each bonus card grants a special ongoing scoring condition or placement benefit for your castles
- Players draft 1 bonus card for each of their 2 castles

#### Other Components
- **Player aids** (1 per player) — summarize room types and scoring
- **Score tracking pad** — for final scoring
- **King token** — determines first player and draft direction
- **Rulebook** with illustrated examples

core_game_loop.content:
#### Semi-Cooperative Castle Building
Between Two Castles of Mad King Ludwig combines the partnership drafting of Between Two Cities with the room-building puzzle of Castles of Mad King Ludwig. Every player simultaneously builds two castles — one shared with the player on their left and one shared with the player on their right. Your final score is the LOWER of your two castles' scores, so you must invest in both equally.

#### Game Flow Overview
The game plays over 2 rounds of tile drafting with a bonus card phase in between:

**Round 1 — Draft from a hand of room tiles**
1. Each player draws a hand of room tiles from a face-down stack.
2. Simultaneously, every player selects 2 tiles from their hand.
3. Each player places 1 tile into their left castle and 1 tile into their right castle, discussing placement with each partner.
4. Pass remaining tiles to the next player (left in Round 1).
5. Repeat until all tiles are drafted.

**Bonus Card Phase**
1. Each player draws bonus cards.
2. Select 1 bonus card for your left castle and 1 for your right castle.
3. Bonus cards provide end-game scoring bonuses or special abilities tied to that castle.

**Round 2 — Draft again with new tiles**
1. Draw a new hand of room tiles.
2. Same drafting process as Round 1, but pass in the opposite direction (right in Round 2).
3. Continue until all tiles are drafted.

#### The Partnership Dynamic
When placing a tile, you must discuss with the partner who shares that castle. Both of you want the castle to score well, but you each have a second castle pulling your attention. Negotiation, compromise, and reading your partners' priorities are essential.

#### Building Rules
- All rooms must connect orthogonally (not diagonally) to the existing castle, building outward from the throne room.
- The castle is built on a grid. Tiles can be placed above, below, left, or right of existing tiles.
- There are no strict size limits, but the castle must remain a single connected structure.
- Rooms placed above the throne room row are considered upper floors; rooms below are basement/ground level.

detailed_rules.content:
#### Setup
1. Place one throne room tile in the center of each building area — one between each pair of adjacent players (so in a 5-player game, there are 5 castles).
2. Shuffle all room tiles face-down and create a supply.
3. Shuffle bonus cards into a face-down deck.
4. Give the King token to a random starting player.
5. Each player receives a player aid.

#### Round 1 Drafting
- Each player draws a hand of tiles from the supply (hand size varies by player count: typically 9 tiles at lower counts, fewer at higher counts).
- Simultaneously, each player picks 2 tiles from their hand, keeping them secret until all players are ready.
- Reveal chosen tiles. Each player places 1 tile into their left castle and 1 tile into their right castle.
- When placing, discuss with your castle partner. You both want the castle to score well, but final placement is a shared decision.
- Pass remaining hand to the left.
- Repeat: pick 2, place 1 in each castle, pass left.
- When hands are too small to continue (2 tiles or fewer remaining), discard leftover tiles face-down.

#### Tile Placement Rules
- Every tile must be placed orthogonally adjacent to at least one existing tile in that castle.
- The castle grows outward from the throne room in a grid pattern.
- Tiles may be placed in any direction (above, below, left, right) as long as connectivity is maintained.
- Once placed, tiles cannot be moved.
- There is no maximum castle size, but each round adds a limited number of tiles.

#### Room Type Scoring Conditions

**Living Rooms (Orange)**
Score based on adjacency. Typical conditions: "2 VP if adjacent to at least 1 sleeping room and 1 food room," or "1 VP per unique room type adjacent to this room."

**Sleeping Rooms (Dark Blue)**
Score based on grouping and position. Typical conditions: "1 VP per sleeping room in a connected group with this room," or "3 VP if this room is on the top floor."

**Food Rooms (Yellow)**
Score based on position relative to other rooms. Typical conditions: "1 VP per room directly above this room," or "2 VP if adjacent to at least 2 different room types."

**Outdoor Rooms (Green)**
Score based on openness and edges. Typical conditions: "1 VP per open side (side not adjacent to another room)," or "2 VP if on the top row of the castle."

**Corridor Rooms (Grey)**
Score based on horizontal/vertical runs and connections. Typical conditions: "1 VP per room in the same row as this room," or "1 VP per corridor in this castle."

**Utility Rooms (Cyan)**
Score based on castle-wide counts. Typical conditions: "1 VP per food room in this castle," or "1 VP per room below the throne room row."

**Downstairs Rooms (Purple)**
Basement-level rooms that score based on depth and quantity. Typical conditions: "2 VP per downstairs room in this castle," or "1 VP for each room directly above this room."

#### Bonus Card Phase (Between Rounds)
- Each player draws a number of bonus cards (varies by player count, typically 2-3).
- Select 1 bonus card to assign to your left castle and 1 to your right castle.
- Discard any extras.
- Bonus cards add end-game scoring conditions to that specific castle (e.g., "3 VP per set of 3 different room types in this castle").
- Bonus cards are public information — all players can see which bonuses each castle has.

#### Round 2 Drafting
- Draw new hands of tiles from the supply.
- Same process as Round 1, but pass hands to the RIGHT instead of left.
- This reversal ensures different tile combinations flow to each player.
- Continue drafting until hands are exhausted.

#### 2-Player Variant
At 2 players, a dummy third castle is built between the players using an automated tile-placement rule. Each player shares one castle with their opponent and one with the dummy. This preserves the "build two castles" dynamic.

#### Larger Player Counts (6-7 Players)
With more players, hand sizes are adjusted to maintain game length. The core rules remain identical — more players means more castles and more negotiation.

scoring_and_endgame.content:
#### Triggering the End
The game ends after Round 2 drafting is complete. There is no variable end condition — every game plays exactly 2 rounds plus the bonus card phase.

#### Castle Scoring
Score each castle individually by summing:

1. **Room tile points** — Evaluate every room tile's scoring condition based on its final position and adjacencies in the castle. Each room scores independently according to its printed condition.
2. **Bonus card points** — Evaluate each castle's bonus card(s) based on the castle's final state.
3. **Room type set bonus** — For each complete set of all 7 room types present in a castle, score bonus VP (typically 5 VP per complete set).
4. **Throne room** — The throne room itself scores 0 VP but is critical for castle structure.

#### Determining Your Score
- Each player has contributed to exactly 2 castles.
- Your personal score equals the LOWER of your two castles' scores.
- This is the critical tension: if you over-invest in one castle at the expense of the other, your score suffers. Balance is everything.

#### Winning
- The player with the highest personal score (i.e., the highest minimum castle score) wins.
- **Tiebreaker**: If tied, the player whose HIGHER castle scored more wins (rewarding overall contribution).
- If still tied, the players share the victory.

#### Typical Score Ranges
- Beginner games: 40-55 VP per castle
- Experienced play: 55-75 VP per castle
- Highly optimized: 75+ VP per castle

#### Scoring Tips
- Count both castles periodically during the game to ensure balance.
- A 70/50 split (personal score: 50) loses to a 60/60 split (personal score: 60). Even investment wins.
- Utility rooms and bonus cards that scale with castle-wide counts tend to be the highest-scoring individual tiles.

beginner_strategy.content:
#### The Golden Rule: Balance Your Two Castles
Your score is the LOWER of your two castles. A lopsided 80/40 split gives you 40 points, while a balanced 60/60 gives you 60. Never neglect either castle. If one castle is pulling ahead, invest your next tile in the weaker one.

#### Room Type Diversity Matters
Try to include all 7 room types in each castle for the set bonus. Even a mediocre room of a missing type is worth including for the 5 VP set bonus.

#### Read Every Tile Before Placing
Each tile's scoring condition is specific. A sleeping room that scores "1 VP per adjacent sleeping room" is wasted if placed with no sleeping room neighbors. Take time to check what each tile actually wants.

#### Utility Rooms Scale Best
Utility rooms that score "1 VP per [type] in this castle" grow in value as the castle gets bigger. Place them early and then build around their condition. A utility room scoring 1 VP per food room is worth 5+ VP in a food-heavy castle.

#### Outdoor Rooms Want Edges
Outdoor rooms often score for open sides (sides not adjacent to other rooms). Place them on the edges or top of your castle, not buried in the middle.

#### Downstairs Rooms Go Below
Downstairs (purple) rooms almost always want to be below the throne room row. Don't place them on the top floor unless their specific condition says otherwise.

#### Communicate With Your Partners
You share each castle with a different player. Talk about what you need. If your left partner is hoarding all the sleeping rooms for your shared castle, tell your right partner you need help with food rooms. Collaboration beats silent play.

#### Common Beginner Mistakes
- **Neglecting one castle** — Your weaker castle IS your score. Check both castles every round.
- **Ignoring adjacency** — Many rooms score based on what is next to them. Random placement wastes points.
- **Hoarding one room type** — A castle with 6 sleeping rooms but no food or outdoor rooms misses set bonuses and has poor scoring diversity.
- **Placing outdoor rooms in the center** — They score for open sides. Surrounded rooms score nothing from that condition.
- **Not reading bonus cards carefully** — Bonus cards can be worth 10+ VP if you build around them. Assign them to the castle that best fits their condition.
- **Fighting your partner** — If your partner wants to place a tile somewhere and you disagree, compromise. A slightly suboptimal placement in a happy partnership beats a perfect placement that causes your partner to abandon your castle.
